title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a sleep researcher called Dr. Simon Vance talking about the importance of sleep for cognitive performance. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a sleep researcher called Dr. Simon Vance talking about the importance of sleep for cognitive performance. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Dr. Simon Vance presents the findings of a recent study on sleep deprivation.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Dr. Simon Vance. Today I’d like to share some insights from our latest research into how sleep—or the lack of it—affects the human brain. We’ve known for a long time that sleep is essential, but our study looked at the specific neurological pathways involved. [7]We recruited a group of volunteers who agreed to stay in our sleep laboratory for a fortnight[/7]. This allowed us to monitor their brain activity with extreme precision. What we found was startling. Even a minor reduction in sleep leads to a significant drop in focus. Interestingly, [8]many participants were unaware that their reaction times had slowed down[/8], believing they were performing at their usual level. One of the most critical functions of sleep is memory consolidation. [9]The brain uses a process called 'neural pruning' to remove unnecessary information[/9] during the night, making room for new learning. Without this, the brain becomes cluttered and inefficient. We also looked at the impact of technology. [10]I was shocked to find that even a short exposure to blue light can delay sleep onset by an hour[/10]. This is particularly relevant for students and office workers. Regarding the environment, [11]the most effective way to improve sleep quality is to ensure the bedroom is kept at a cool temperature[/11]. Many people overlook this simple factor. During the experiment, [12]seeing the rapid recovery of cognitive function after just one night of 'catch-up' sleep was truly remarkable[/12]. It shows how resilient the brain can be. However, long-term deprivation is another story. [13]I believe that sleep health should be a mandatory part of the school curriculum[/13] to educate the next generation. As we often tell our patients, [14]sleep is not a luxury, but a 'biological necessity' for a healthy life[/14].

dialogue.speaker: simon
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Dr. Simon Vance. Today I’d like to share some insights from our latest research into how sleep—or the lack of it—affects the human brain. We’ve known for a long time that sleep is essential, but our study looked at the specific neurological pathways involved. We recruited a group of volunteers who agreed to stay in our sleep laboratory for a fortnight. This allowed us to monitor their brain activity with extreme precision. What we found was startling. Even a minor reduction in sleep leads to a significant drop in focus. Interestingly, many participants were unaware that their reaction times had slowed down, believing they were performing at their usual level. One of the most critical functions of sleep is memory consolidation. The brain uses a process called 'neural pruning' to remove unnecessary information during the night, making room for new learning. Without this, the brain becomes cluttered and inefficient. We also looked at the impact of technology. I was shocked to find that even a short exposure to blue light can delay sleep onset by an hour. This is particularly relevant for students and office workers. Regarding the environment, the most effective way to improve sleep quality is to ensure the bedroom is kept at a cool temperature. Many people overlook this simple factor. During the experiment, seeing the rapid recovery of cognitive function after just one night of 'catch-up' sleep was truly remarkable. It shows how resilient the brain can be. However, long-term deprivation is another story. I believe that sleep health should be a mandatory part of the school curriculum to educate the next generation. As we often tell our patients, sleep is not a luxury, but a 'biological necessity' for a healthy life.

questions.question: Dr. Vance uses the term (9) to describe how the brain filters out useless data during sleep.
questions.explanation: He uses this specific term to describe the clearing of information.

questions.question: According to the research, (10) can have a negative effect on how quickly people fall asleep.
questions.explanation: Short exposure to blue light can delay sleep onset.

questions.question: Dr. Vance recommends maintaining a (11) in the bedroom to help people sleep better.
questions.explanation: Keeping the bedroom at a cool temperature is identified as the most effective way.

questions.question: The researchers were impressed by the speed of (12) once the volunteers were allowed to sleep normally again.
questions.explanation: He mentions the rapid recovery of cognitive function was remarkable.

questions.question: Dr. Vance suggests that information about sleep should be included in the (13) .
questions.explanation: He believes sleep health should be part of the school curriculum.

questions.question: Dr. Vance refers to sleep as a (14) to emphasize that it is essential for survival.
questions.explanation: He concludes by calling sleep a 'biological necessity'.
